identity.name: MoMA — Marcel Duchamp Retrospective
identity.address: 11 W 53rd St, New York, NY 10019, USA
identity.location: 11 W 53rd St, New York, NY 10019, USA

narrative.summary:
𝗔𝗥𝗥𝗜𝗩𝗔𝗟

53rd St between 5th and 6th — the temple of modern art.

The first major North American Duchamp retrospective in over 50 years. Nearly 300 works spanning six decades. Opens April 12, 2026.

blocks.title: Arrival
blocks.body:
𝗔𝗥𝗥𝗜𝗩𝗔𝗟

11 W 53rd St — the Steven and Alexandra Cohen Center for Special Exhibitions.

"Why is this art?" — the question that hovers over every corner of contemporary art is virtually impossible to answer without Marcel Duchamp. This is the show that reframes everything. Nearly 300 works across all mediums, spanning Duchamp's six-decade career. The last North American retrospective of this scale was over 50 years ago. If you care about why art looks the way it does today, this is pilgrimage.

blocks.title: The Exhibition
blocks.body:
𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗘𝗫𝗛𝗜𝗕𝗜𝗧𝗜𝗢𝗡

Duchamp (1887–1968) — the man who put a urinal in a gallery and called it art. But that reductive reading misses the depth. This retrospective traces his evolution from early Cubist paintings through the readymades, the Large Glass, the chess obsession, and into Étant donnés — the secret installation he worked on for 20 years.

Expect to see:
• Nude Descending a Staircase (No. 2) ⭐
• Fountain (1917) — the readymade that changed everything
• The Bride Stripped Bare by Her Bachelors, Even (The Large Glass) ⭐
• Bicycle Wheel, Bottle Rack, and other readymades
• Chess sets and correspondence
• Works across painting, sculpture, film, photography, and ephemera

For John: Duchamp sits at the intersection of your interests — the existentialist questioning, the Cubist roots, the radical reimagining of what art can be. This is essential.

blocks.title: Check
blocks.body:
𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗖𝗛𝗘𝗖𝗞

💰 Admission: $30 adults, $22 seniors, $17 students. Free Fridays 4-8pm (first-come)

🌐 moma.org/calendar/exhibitions/5820
📞 (212) 708-9400

𝗢𝗡 𝗩𝗜𝗘𝗪: April 12 – August 22, 2026

𝗛𝗢𝗨𝗥𝗦
Mon–Thu, Sat–Sun: 10:30 AM – 5:30 PM
Friday: 10:30 AM – 8:30 PM

𝗕𝗘𝗦𝗧 𝗧𝗜𝗠𝗘𝗦
• Weekday morning: Smallest crowds, most contemplative
• Friday evening (4-8pm): Free admission but crowded
• Allow 2-3 hours for this show — 300 works demands time
• Combine with the permanent collection (Starry Night, Demoiselles d'Avignon)
